data.title: GOP frustrations grow over lackluster ‘big, beautiful bill’ sales pitch
data.description:
Republicans are growing increasingly concerned that they’re behind the eight ball in selling the One Big Beautiful Bill Act as the party’s top legislative achievement. 

Talk about the massive tax package largely evaporated after Labor Day amid high-stakes fights over releasing documents related to Jeffrey Epstein and the 43-day government shutdown. Yet even before those controversies, polls showed the GOP legislation was unpopular. 

This has frustrated lawmakers, who say the party needs to do more to sell its signature accomplishment, especially as Democrats hammer them on the issue of affordability. 

“No,” Sen. John Cornyn (R-Texas) said when asked whether he was satisfied with the party’s sales job in recent months. “There’s so much good stuff in there, and I just think it’s inherently difficult to sell something that has that many moving parts and is that complex.” 

“We hopefully learned a lesson that while the ‘One Big, Beautiful Bill [Act]’ doesn’t poll well by that name, if you change it to the ‘Working Family’s Tax Cuts Act,’ it polls much better,” Cornyn said, referring to the GOP’s attempted rebranding of the bill’s name dating back to near Labor Day. “So we need to go back to Marketing 101, I guess.”

The grandiose package passed just before July 4, and party leaders urged lawmakers to use the August recess to incessantly pitch the plan — with its permanent extension of the 2017 tax cuts — back home. 

But since then, the measure has largely gotten buried. Capitol Hill moved on swiftly upon its return after Labor Day to the looming shutdown fight that lasted nearly two months and the prolonged battle over Epstein that helped paralyze the House at times. 

There has also been scant public polling of the bill since mid-September, with lawmakers indicating constituents have other concerns on their minds. 

When asked how much the tax package comes up on the campaign trail with voters, Sen. Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.), who is running to become the next governor of Alabama in 2027, replied: “Not much.” 

“Most people don’t really understand what’s in it,” he said. 

Complicating the picture for the GOP is a sluggish economic backdrop, headlined by inflation that has ticked upward and tariffs that have contributed to higher prices. 

Trump on Tuesday labeled the Democratic affordability chatter a “con job,” even as his approval rating slips amid voter concerns about his handling of the economy.

“It’s way too f‑‑‑ing expensive to live in this country right now,” one GOP operative said. “We need to be seen as at least caring about that.” 

Lawmakers and operatives point to a series of problems that have plagued the attempted sales job in recent months, including the White House’s flood-the-zone messaging that has helped prevent any single subject from truly emerging.

The party, though, is starting to shift back into sales mode ahead of the calendar turn to 2026, during which many of the provisions included in the bill will finally go into effect. 

Senate GOP leaders this week have repeatedly tried to highlight portions of the bill, including the tax cut extension and investments aimed at bolstering the border and the air traffic control system, among other things. Talk about the need to sell the bill at the state level also came up during their weekly policy luncheon on Tuesday, with lawmakers set to have substantially more time to do that as they are set to spend less time in Washington next year.

“I think it is one of the most … undertold [stories], and we need to change that,” Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told The Hill. 

“We’ve been bogged down with other stuff,” he continued. “But now when we get into an election year and we’re drawing the contrast and making the argument for why to keep and hopefully expand our majorities, all of the components and pieces of that are going to be part of that argument.” 

It’s not just the Senate side that’s beating the drum for outreach on the package to get ratcheted up. 

Rep. Richard Hudson (R-N.C.), the chair of the House GOP’s campaign arm, reportedly told lawmakers this week that more work has to be done in the coming weeks and months to sell the bill and popular components of it, including no tax on tips, the expansion of the child tax credit and the tax cut extension. 

House Republicans are staring down warning signs that their slim majority could be in peril next year, especially after the party pulled off a House special election win in Tennessee on Tuesday night by single digits in a district President Trump carried by more than 20 points last year. 

John McLaughlin, a longtime pollster for the president, wrote recently in an op-ed detailing a new poll by his firm that “most voters remain confused” about the legislation. 

Forty-two percent of respondents said they view the One Big Beautiful Bill Act as a tax cut, while 37 percent said they see it as a spending bill. He also labeled the 46 percent support for the cuts as “tepid.” 

By contrast, Democrats have used the past three months to drive home their health care message ahead of the expiration of the enhanced Affordable Care Act credits at year’s end. That effort dovetails with the Medicaid cuts that were included in the GOP’s top legislative item. 

Another headache for the GOP and their sales pitch is Trump, who has not talked about the tax bill as much as some in the party would like and has largely eschewed political rallies in key states that previously have been one of his hallmarks. 

“Has the president gone to a single event about OBBBA?” the operative fumed, using an acronym for the bill. “Have they gone to Toledo? Have they gone to Scranton? … I don’t know — use the bully pulpit for a minute while we’re at it? Would be nice.” 

Multiple Senate GOP members said they expect Trump to ramp up that travel and talk in the new year, something they view as an absolute imperative to get the message out. 

“He’s gotten diverted a lot into international [affairs],” said Sen. Shelley Moore Capito (R-W.Va.), a member of GOP leadership. “I think he will as we turn toward the new year, but I do think that’s left a vacuum.”

“I think we could do better,” Capito said about the overall selling of the legislation. “I wouldn’t give us an ‘A.’”

data.title: Here’s where redistricting stands in key states
data.description:
The redistricting wars show no signs of waning as several more states move toward rewriting their congressional lines.  

Florida, Indiana and Virginia are among the states taking steps to redraw their maps ahead of the 2026 midterms, the latest phase in an ongoing redistricting battle across the country. 

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court is expected to weigh in on whether Texas can use its new GOP-friendly House map in time for next year. 

Here’s a look at where redistricting stands in these states: 

Texas 

The Lone Star State passed a new set of congressional lines this summer that aims to give the GOP up to five pickup opportunities next year, a move that was made after pressure from President Trump and his allies.

The map was temporarily put on hold after a federal panel of judges in Texas ruled that the new map was likely a racial gerrymander. Republicans then appealed to the Supreme Court, where Justice Samuel Alito granted a temporary stay, allowing the map to go into effect while the top court prepares to issue a final decision.

With the Monday candidate filing deadline in Texas rapidly approaching, that ruling is expected soon.

California 

California voters overwhelmingly passed Proposition 50 last month, creating a new Democratic-friendly House map for the midterm elections that would give the party up to five pickup opportunities.  

California Democrats teed up the ballot measure for a November special election in response to Texas Republicans’ moves to advance their GOP-friendly map. 

The GOP filed a lawsuit quickly after the election, which the Trump administration has joined onto, alleging Democrats “engaged in unconstitutional racial gerrymandering” and that state lawmakers violated the Constitution “when it drew new congressional district lines based on race, specifically to favor Hispanic voters, without cause or evidence to justify it.” 

A federal panel of judges will hold a hearing over the case on Dec. 15. 

Indiana  

Indiana Republicans have been under pressure from Trump and his allies to pass a new set of district lines, though some members of the party have continued to resist those efforts.

An Indiana House panel advanced a new map Tuesday, just one day after those lines were unveiled. The new map now goes before the House for a full vote.

The Indiana Senate is expected to convene Monday to make a “final decision” over any redistricting proposals put forward by the state House. But it remains unclear whether enough Senate Republicans will support the new map. 

Virginia 

Virginia Democrats have begun the process of moving forward with their redrawn map.

They passed a mid-decade redistricting bill in October that allowed lawmakers to initiate the process. Lawmakers need to pass it one more time in the state Legislature, which they are expected to do easily.

That vote will tee up a constitutional amendment before voters for a special election, which they’re aiming to do in the spring or summer. A new map is expected to create at least two additional Democratic-leaning districts.

Several Republican efforts to block Democrats from advancing their redistricting ballot initiative have been unsuccessful so far. 

Missouri 

Missouri Republicans also passed a new House map earlier this year amid a push from the White House and national Republicans, dismantling Rep. Emanuel Cleaver’s (D-Mo.) district to give the GOP one additional pickup opportunity. 

Opponents are working to thwart the new map through litigation and the referendum process. A judge is supposed to rule by Tuesday whether the referendum can advance.

North Carolina 

North Carolina Republicans advanced a new congressional map in October, weakening Rep. Don Davis’s (D-N.C.) seat in the state’s battleground 1st Congressional District.

A federal judge panel ruled last month that the map could be used during the 2026 cycle after opponents looked to block the map from going into effect before the midterms. 

Utah 

A Utah judge handed a win to Democrats last month by choosing a House map for 2026 that gives the party one pickup opportunity.  

Earlier this summer, 3rd District Court Judge Dianna Gibson ordered Republicans to redraw their congressional map, arguing they erred in going around an initiative approved by voters in 2018 that reformed the redistricting process to pass a map favorable to the GOP.

Florida 

A Florida House redistricting panel is set to convene Thursday for its first meeting as Republicans look to move forward with new maps.  

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) said in an interview with The Floridian this week that he is planning to call a special session, likely sometime between March and May, to do redistricting.

Ohio 

Ohio lawmakers struck a deal in October that will make two Democratic-held seats — held by Rep. Greg Landsman in the 1st District and Rep. Marcy Kaptur in the 9th District — more Republican-friendly, while making Rep. Emilia Sykes’s (D) seat in the 13th District a little bluer. 

Lawmakers were forced to draw new maps after the last set were passed without enough bipartisan support, meaning they could only be in place for four years and not the entire decade. 

Maryland 

Maryland Gov. Wes Moore (D) has signaled he wants to move forward with redistricting, but the effort faces hurdles given Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson’s (D) opposition to the move.  

Maryland Democrats tried to pass an aggressive 8-0 map after the 2020 U.S. Census, but it was later struck down in the courts in 2022 as a partisan gerrymander — a risk that Democrats could be contending with again if they draw new maps again. 

New York

Voters filed a lawsuit in New York in October over Rep. Nicole Malliotakis’s (R-N.Y.) seat, arguing that “Black and Latino Staten Islanders have less opportunity than other members of the electorate to elect a representative of their choice and influence elections in New York’s 11th Congressional District,” violating New York’s constitution.   

A lawsuit would present Democrats’ best chance of redrawing their map in the Empire State ahead of 2026 given the party would otherwise need to pass a constitutional amendment to allow New York to sidestep its redistricting commission. But even in the latter scenario, such a move would mean that Democrats would only be able to draw a new map in time for the 2028 elections.

data.title: Hegseth in hot water amid fallout from boat strike
Noem says she met with Trump, recommended full travel ban
data.description:
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said that she recommended a “full travel ban” to President Trump during a Monday meeting. 

“I just met with the President. I am recommending a full travel ban on every damn country that’s been flooding our nation with killers, leeches, and entitlement junkies,” Noem said Monday on the social platform X.

“Our forefathers built this nation on blood, sweat, and the unyielding love of freedom—not for foreign invaders to slaughter our heroes, suck dry our hard-earned tax dollars, or snatch the benefits owed to AMERICANS,” she continued. “WE DON’T WANT THEM. NOT ONE.”

In the wake of the shooting of two West Virginia National Guard members in Washington, D.C., last Wednesday, the Trump administration has cracked down on migration. 

U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) indefinitely paused the processing of immigration requests from Afghans on Wednesday. Rahmanullah Lakanwal, the suspected shooter of U.S. Army Spc. Sarah Beckstrom and U.S. Air Force Staff Sgt. Andrew Wolfe, is Aghan national who entered the country in 2021 via a program for those who assisted the U.S. during its two-decade war in the Middle Eastern nation.

Beckstrom, 20, died from her wounds on Thursday, while Wolfe, 24, remains in serious condition after showing “positive” signs, West Virginia Gov. Patrick Morrisey (R) said Monday.

USCIS Director Joseph Edlow also said Thursday that his agency, at the direction of the president, is reexamining green cards held by individuals from 19 countries. 

A June memo from the White House restricted migration from 19 countries of concern: Afghanistan, Burundi, Chad, the Republic of Congo, Cuba, Equatorial Guinea, Eritrea, Haiti, Iran, Laos, Libya, Myanmar, Sierra Leone, Somalia, Sudan, Togo, Turkmenistan, Venezuela and Yemen.

When reached, the Department of Homeland Security said the full list of countries impacted by Noem’s recommendation will be revealed soon. The Hill has also reached out to the White House for comment.

data.title: Trump fires back at Walz over MRI comments: ‘If they want to release it, it’s okay’
data.description:
President Trump on Sunday took a swing at Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) over comments the governor made in which he pressed the president to “release the MRI results.”

“Governor Walz asked you to release the MRI records,” a reporter on Air Force One noted to Trump.

“Governor Walz? You mean, the incompetent governor? So, if they want to release it, it’s okay with me to release it,” Trump replied.

Walz recently returned fire at Trump referencing the Minnesota governor in a rant on Thanksgiving. 

“Release the MRI results,” Walz said on the social platform X on Friday, as the president said in October he went through an MRI during a visit to Walter Reed National Military Medical Center.

Trump went after Walz, used a slur against him and said the governor “does nothing” on the number of Somalian refugees in Minnesota in a Thursday post on his Truth Social platform.

“Hundreds of thousands of refugees from Somalia are completely taking over the once great State of Minnesota,” Trump said. “Somalian gangs are roving the streets looking for ‘prey’ as our wonderful people stay locked in their apartments and houses hoping against hope that they will be left alone.”

Walz said Sunday that Trump was “demonizing” Somali migrants in Minnesota.

“As far as demonizing our Somali community, maybe, he could help us on some things,” Walz told host Kristen Welker on NBC’s “Meet the Press.” “Demonizing an entire community, folks who are in the professions, educators, artists, doctors, lawyers, entrepreneurs, they bring the diversity and the energy to a place like Minnesota.”

The Hill has reached out to Walz’s office for comment.

data.title: Post-Thanksgiving flight delays could continue into beginning of week
data.description:
Widespread post-Thanksgiving flight delays due to winter weather could continue through the beginning of the week, according to officials.

There were 11,273 flight delays within, into or out of the United States on Sunday, according to the flight-tracking website FlightAware, with 831 cancellations within, into or out of the country.

The recent delays also follow air travel chaos caused by the recent record-breaking government shutdown, which lasted more than a month.

In a Sunday post on the social platform X the National Weather Service’s (NWS) Weather Prediction Center said that “a winter storm will approach the Mid Atlantic and Northeast beginning Monday evening with a threat of snow and ice across the interior portions of both regions.”

“Any snowfall and ice will present hazardous travel concerns, especially across the interior Northeast U.S.,” the post continued.

Chicago O’Hare International Airport, which had been prepping for the busiest Thanksgiving travel week in its history, experienced “severe winter weather” that it said was “impacting flights” at the airport Saturday.

“Cancellations and delays have begun as a winter storm moves through the area and is expected to continue for much of Saturday,” the airport, which experienced 529 flight delays for departures and 533 delays for arrivals that day, per FlightAware.

An NWS forecast office in Chicago had said early Saturday on X that “a winter storm” was “still expected to bring widespread accumulating snow and travel impacts to the region today and tonight!”

data.title: Bacon says Witkoff ‘cannot be trusted to lead’ Russia-Ukraine peace negotiations
data.description:
Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.) said Tuesday that Special Envoy Steve Witkoff “cannot be trusted” to lead negotiations between the administration, Russia and Ukraine.

“For those who oppose the Russian invasion and want to see Ukraine prevail as a sovereign & democratic country, it is clear that Witkoff fully favors the Russians,” said Bacon, a staunch backer of Ukraine, on the social platform X. “He cannot be trusted to lead these negotiations.

“Would a Russian paid agent do less than he? He should be fired.”

Bacon’s remarks came hours after Bloomberg reported that Witkoff instructed a Russian official on how Russian President Vladimir Putin should propose a peace plan to President Trump. It also came hours before Trump said that his envoy will likely meet with the Russian leader in Moscow next week.

During an Oct. 14 conversation, Witkoff reportedly told Yuri Ushakov, a foreign policy aide to Putin, that he thinks the three sides can agree to a plan similar to the 20-point proposal agreed to by Israel and Hamas days earlier. 

Witkoff also told Ushakov to direct Putin to congratulate Trump on brokering the deal in the Middle East and to call the president “a man of peace.”

Trump and Putin spoke via phone on Oct. 16, a day before Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky met with his American counterpart at the White House. 

The president told reporters on Air Force One on Tuesday that while he was unaware of the phone call, it is “standard” practice.

“[Witkoff has] got to sell this Ukraine, he’s got to sell Ukraine to Russia, that’s what a deal maker does,” Trump said.

The president added that Witkoff, and perhaps his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, will likely meet with Putin in Moscow after Thanksgiving. The Hill has reached out to the State Department for comment on whether Secretary of State Marco Rubio will also attend. 

Witkoff, Kushner and Rubio led a U.S. delegation in negotiations with Ukrainian officials in Geneva over the weekend, as the two sides — with input from European leaders — ironed out the details of a peace proposal backed by the Trump administration.

Earlier Tuesday, Rustem Umerov, secretary of Ukraine’s National Security and Defense Council, said on X that the Ukrainian and U.S. delegations agreed on the “core terms” of the proposal.

data.title: GOP rep calls to stop ‘ridiculous side shows’ amid Russia-Ukraine peace deal talks
data.description:
Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) believes Special Envoy Steve Witkoff instructing a Russian official on how to suggest a peace plan to President Trump is a “major problem.”

The transcript of an Oct. 14 phone call between Witkoff and Yuri Ushakov, a foreign policy aide to Russian President Vladimir Putin, was reported by Bloomberg on Tuesday. It comes as Ukraine agreed to “core terms” of a peace proposal outlined by the Trump administration earlier in the day.

“This is a major problem,” Fitzpatrick said Tuesday on the social platform X. “And one of the many reasons why these ridiculous side shows and secret meetings need to stop.” 

The Pennsylvania Republican added that the administration should allow Secretary of State Marco Rubio to “do his job in a fair and objective manner.”

Since returning to office, President Trump has dispatched Witkoff and Rubio to negotiate with their Russian and Ukrainian counterparts to bring an end to the ongoing conflict. 

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

During their conversation, Witkoff tells Ushakov that he thinks the three sides can agree to a plan similar to the 20-point proposal offered by the administration, which Israel and Hamas agreed to days earlier. 

Witkoff also tells Ushakov to tell Putin to congratulate Trump on brokering the deal in the Middle East and to call the president “a man of peace.” 

Trump and Putin spoke by phone on Oct. 16, a day before Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky met with his American counterpart at the White House. 

On Tuesday morning, Rustem Umerov, the secretary of Ukraine’s National Security and Defense, said on X that the U.S. and Ukrainian delegations “reached a common understanding on the core terms” of the plan discussed in recent days in Geneva.

The original peace proposal, crafted by U.S. and Russian officials, called for — among other items — confirming Ukraine’s sovereignty and providing it with security guarantees from the U.S. Russia, meanwhile, would enshrine a nonaggression policy toward Europe and Ukraine.

The plan, though, has been tweaked after input from European leaders.

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Trump says Witkoff likely to meet with Putin next week
data.description:
President Trump said Tuesday that Special Envoy Steve Witkoff will likely meet with Russian President Vladimir Putin in Moscow next week, as the administration looks to broker an end to the war in Ukraine. 

“I think so,” Trump told reporters on Air Force One on whether Witkoff will meet with the Russian leader. 

Witkoff last met with Putin in Moscow in August. The president added that his son-in-law, Jared Kushner, may also join. 

Witkoff, Kushner, and Secretary of State Marco Rubio were part of the U.S. delegation that met with Ukrainian officials in Geneva over the weekend. The Hill has reached out to the State Department regarding whether Rubio will also be part of the Moscow delegation.

In Geneva, U.S. and Ukrainian officials, with input from European leaders, ironed out the details of a peace proposal from the Trump administration. 

The original plan, crafted by U.S. and Russian officials, called for — among other items — confirming Ukraine’s sovereignty, providing it with security guarantees from the U.S., and ensuring that Russia adopts a nonaggression policy toward Ukraine. 

The proposal has been tweaked amid negotiations in Switzerland, however.

Earlier Tuesday, Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said in a video on the social platform X that his negotiators are working with the U.S. to “ensure truly real, effective steps toward ending this war.”

Bloomberg also reported Tuesday on an Oct. 14 phone call between the U.S. special envoy and Yuri Usakov, a foreign policy aide to Putin.

During their conversation, Witkoff tells Ushakov that he thinks the three sides can agree to a plan similar to the 20-point outline Israel and Hamas agreed to days earlier. 

Witkoff also tells Ushakov to tell Putin to congratulate Trump on brokering the deal in the Middle East and to call the president a “man of peace.”

Trump and Putin spoke via phone on Oct. 16, a day before Zelensky met with his American counterpart at the White House. 

The president told reporters Tuesday that, while he had not previously heard of the phone call, it is a “standard thing,” and Witkoff likely gave similar instructions to Ukrainian officials. 

“He’s got to sell this to Ukraine, he’s got to sell Ukraine to Russia, that’s what a deal maker does,” Trump added.

Overnight on Tuesday, Russia struck residential buildings and civilian infrastructure in Kyiv, killing six and injuring 13, Zelensky said on X. The Ukrainian leader noted that Russia also struck the Odesa, Dnipro, Kharkiv, Chernihiv and Cherkasy regions. 

Libbey Dean and The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Johnson counters Thune on Russian sanctions bill process: ‘Not my understanding’
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said that Senate Majority Leader John Thune’s (R-S.D.) assertion that a bill to expand Russian sanctions would start in the House came as a surprise to him, countering the Senate Republican leader by saying it would be “simpler and quicker” to advance the bill if it first passes in the Senate.

“That was not my understanding up until yesterday,” Johnson said in an interview with The Hill on Friday, responding to Thune’s comments.

“It is simpler and quicker to get it done if it comes from the Senate to the House with a large vote margin, which we expect,” Johnson said.

Thune was asked about the status of the sanctions bill in a Wednesday press conference after President Trump and the White House had signaled support for Russian sanctions legislation.

“I think what is more likely happens is the House originates that,” Thune said. “It’s a revenue measure, and so it has to — you know, revenue measures originate in the House. If we had one available to us in the Senate we could do it here.”

“But I think too, if you want to expedite movement of that in terms of getting it on the president’s desk, it’s probably quicker if it comes out of the House, comes over to us, and we’re able to take it up and process it on the floor,” Thune said. 

President Trump told reporters last weekend that it was “OK with me” if Congress moved on sanctions legislation, and Reuters reported a senior White House official said the president is willing to sign such a bill.  

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) has been the vocal leader of the Sanctioning Russia Act of 2025, which has supermajority support in the Senate with 84 cosponsors, and has repeatedly said such legislation would pass quickly.

Johnson said he believes the bill should start in the Senate, where he expected it would get 85 or 90 votes.

“The White House is as well as the President has said, the fastest way for us to get that done is for the bill to come from the Senate to the House, and not the other way around,” Johnson told The Hill.

“To process it through the House, it would go to as many as seven committees of jurisdiction. — you know, seven different committee processes,” Johnson said. “And even if we could convince a few of those chairmen to waive their jurisdiction, others are going to want to dig in on the policy. So, it would be a much easier prospect and a much quicker result to have it come from the Senate to the House.”

“It has been my understanding for months that that is what we were to expect,” Johnson said, adding that he would talk to Thune soon about the process.

The move comes as President Trump is pushing a 28-point peace proposal to end the war in Ukraine that has gotten some criticism from being too differential to Russia.

Even as he calls on the Senate to go first, Johnson is set to return from Thanksgiving to an effort by a House Republican to force a vote on the sanctions bill in the lower chamber.

Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) on Friday said he would file a discharge petition to force a vote on the House version of the sanctions bill. Fitzpatrick would need at least 218 signatures — necessitating support from at least a handful of Republicans — before he could force such a vote.

The Russian sanctions bill is just the latest issue on which Johnson and Thune have been out of sync in the last two weeks.

Johnson was blindsided by the Senate inserting a provision into a bill to re-open the federal government that would allow senators to sue for substantial sums if their electronic records were obtained without notification, as a response to former special counsel Jack Smith’s Arctic Frost investigation. The House this week unanimously passed a bill to repeal the provision, but it is not expected to advance in the Senate.

The Speaker also asserted confidence this week that the Senate would amend a bill directing the Department of Justice to release files related to sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, which Johnson was forced to bring up after a majority of members signed a discharge petition. The Senate, though, promptly passed the bill as-is by unanimous consent just hours later.
